title: Transformers
lesson:
Almost every modern AI model you've heard of — the ones behind ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and Llama — is built on one design called the Transformer. It was introduced in a 2017 research paper with the now-famous title "Attention Is All You Need". The Transformer is not a single program; it's an architecture — a blueprint for how to wire up the parts of a neural network (a system of simple math units, loosely inspired by brain cells, that learn patterns from data). When people say "GPT" they literally mean Generative Pre-trained Transformer. So if you only learn one piece of AI plumbing, make it this one.

The problem it solved: reading in parallel

Before Transformers, the leading text models read words one at a time, left to right, like a person sounding out a sentence syllable by syllable. That was slow and forgetful — by the end of a long paragraph, the model had half-forgotten the start. The Transformer's big trick is that it reads every token at once, in parallel, and lets each token look at every other token to decide what matters. (A token is the small chunk of text a model actually processes — see Tokens.)

💡 Think of a busy kitchen. The old approach was one chef cooking each dish start-to-finish before touching the next. The Transformer is a brigade: every station works at the same time, and they constantly glance at each other to stay in sync. Same meal, far faster, and nothing gets forgotten on the back burner.

What's inside the box

A Transformer is a tall stack of identical layers (a layer is one processing step; a big model might stack 30 to 100 of them). Information flows up through the stack, getting richer at each step. Each layer has two main tools:

  • Attention. This is the part that lets each token gather context from the others — figuring out, for example, that in "the trophy didn't fit in the suitcase because it was too big", the word "it" refers to the trophy. Attention is so central it gets its own lesson: Attention Mechanism.
  • A feed-forward network. After attention mixes information between tokens, this part does private thinking on each token on its own — a small math function that reshapes the numbers, like each chef refining their own plate after the group huddle.

Wrapped around these are two helpers that keep the tall stack stable: residual connections (a shortcut that adds a layer's input back onto its output, so information can skip ahead and isn't lost) and layer normalization (rescaling the numbers so they don't balloon or vanish as they climb the stack).

The librarian analogy for attention

Here's a second picture. Imagine each token is a researcher who shouts a query: "who here is about animals?" Every other token holds up a key label describing itself, and a value — the actual information it carries. The researcher listens hardest to the tokens whose key best matches the query, and blends their values together. That query–key–value matching is attention. Stack many of these and the model builds a deep, context-aware understanding of the whole sentence. The numbers each token carries are its Embeddings — meaning expressed as a long list of numbers.

One small problem: order

Because the Transformer looks at all tokens at once, it has no built-in sense of order — to it, "dog bites man" and "man bites dog" would look the same. The fix is positional encoding: a position tag added to each token so the model knows token 1 came before token 5. It's like numbering the pages of a book you tore apart and shuffled — now you can still read it in order.

Two flavours you'll meet

The original Transformer had two halves: an encoder (reads and understands input) and a decoder (writes output). Most chat models today are decoder-only — they just predict the next token, over and over. A tiny sketch of how you actually run one:

from transformers import pipeline

# Loads a small decoder-only Transformer and predicts the next tokens
gen = pipeline("text-generation", model="gpt2")
out = gen("The kitchen brigade works in", max_new_tokens=10)
print(out[0]["generated_text"])

That transformers library (from Hugging Face) is the standard toolkit for loading these models. Under the hood it runs the exact attention-plus-feed-forward stack described above. The knobs the model learned during training — billions of numbers — are its Parameters, and the limit on how many tokens it can attend to at once is its Context Windows. To see how all this fits the bigger picture of generating text, revisit How AI Models Work.
mentalModel: A Transformer is a kitchen brigade, not a single chef: every token-station works at once and constantly glances at the others (that's attention) to keep the whole dish in sync — then the stack repeats this huddle dozens of times, getting smarter at each layer.
mistakes: Thinking "Transformer" means ChatGPT. The Transformer is the underlying blueprint (the architecture). ChatGPT, Claude, and Llama are specific models built on that blueprint — like "car engine" versus a particular car.

Believing the model reads left-to-right like a person. Transformers read all tokens in parallel and use positional encoding to recover word order. Forgetting this makes the whole "attention" idea confusing.

Confusing attention with the feed-forward part. Attention mixes information between tokens; the feed-forward network processes each token on its own. They alternate, and both matter — skipping either breaks the model.
Assuming more layers always means smarter. Depth helps only up to a point and costs memory and speed. Beyond that you need more data, better training, or a different design — not just a taller stack.

Mixing up the architecture with its size. A 1-billion and a 400-billion parameter model can share the same Transformer design; the difference is how many learned numbers (and how much training) each has.

Ignoring that attention cost grows fast. Comparing every token to every other token scales roughly with the square of the sequence length, which is why long inputs get slow and expensive — and why tricks like Flash Attention exist.
exercise:
Install Hugging Face's library (pip install transformers torch) and run the small gpt2 snippet from the lesson. Give it three different prompts and note how the next tokens change. Then, in plain English, write two sentences: one describing what the attention part is doing for your prompt, and one describing what the feed-forward part adds.

Stretch goal: Set output_attentions=True when loading the model, run one short sentence through it, and print the attention scores for a single layer. Find the word each token "pays most attention to" and check whether it lines up with what you'd expect grammatically (e.g. does a pronoun attend to the noun it refers to?).
solution: For the main task you should see gpt2 continue each prompt differently — proof the same Transformer produces different output depending on input. A good answer says something like: "Attention let the model look back at earlier words (e.g. linking 'it' to 'trophy') to gather context, while the feed-forward step refined each token's own representation in isolation." For the stretch goal, attention scores come back as a grid of numbers (one row per token); the highest value in a row tells you which other token that token focused on. Pronouns and verbs often attend to their related nouns, but don't expect it to be perfectly tidy — each of the many attention 'heads' learns its own pattern, and not all of them are human-readable.
summary: The Transformer is the blueprint behind nearly every modern LLM. Its breakthrough is reading all tokens at once and using attention so each token can pull context from every other, while a feed-forward step refines each token alone — repeated across a tall stack of layers. Positional encoding restores word order. Most chat models are decoder-only Transformers that simply predict the next token, and the design's main cost is that attention scales with the square of the input length.

keyTerms.def: The neural-network architecture (blueprint) behind nearly all modern LLMs. It reads all tokens in parallel and uses attention to share context between them.
The overall design or wiring of a model — how its parts connect — separate from its size or the specific data it was trained on.
One processing step in the stack. Each Transformer layer contains an attention part and a feed-forward part; big models stack dozens to a hundred of them.
The mechanism that lets each token look at every other token and decide which ones matter, using query–key–value matching.
The part of each layer that processes every token on its own after attention has mixed information between them.
Extra position information added to each token so the model knows word order, since it otherwise reads all tokens at once with no sense of sequence.
A shortcut that adds a layer's input back onto its output, letting information skip ahead so it isn't lost in a deep stack.
A Transformer that only generates output by predicting the next token repeatedly. Most chat models (GPT, Llama) work this way.
